name: Janet Gaynor
bio: Janet Gaynor was born Laura Gainor on October 6, 1906, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. She managed to land bit parts in several early films before turning in acclaimed performances in 7th Heaven and Sunrise. Based on those films plus Street Angel, Janet received the very first Academy Award for Best Actress in 1929. When talkies replaced silent films, Janet was one of the few who made a successful transition. She received another Oscar nomination for A Star Is Born in 1937. She passed away on September 14, 1984, in Palm Springs, California, at the age of 77.

name: Fredric March
bio: Fredric March was born Ernest Frederick McIntyre Bickel on August 31, 1897, in Racine, Wisconsin. He is one of the few actors to have won two Academy Awards for Best Actor, taking home the prize for Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde in 1932 and The Best Years of Our Lives in 1947. Known for his versatility across drama, comedy, and tragedy, March had a long and distinguished career on both stage and screen. He passed away on April 14, 1975, in Los Angeles, California.
movies: The Best Years of Our Lives

name: John Wayne
bio: John Wayne was born Marion Robert Morrison on May 26, 1907, in Winterset, Iowa. One of the most iconic figures in Hollywood history, Wayne became synonymous with the American Western genre. He appeared in over 170 films across a career spanning five decades. He won the Academy Award for Best Actor for True Grit in 1969. Known for his distinctive voice, walk, and screen presence, Wayne remains one of the top box office draws in film history. He passed away on June 11, 1979, in Los Angeles, California.

name: Vincent Price
bio: Vincent Price was born on May 27, 1911, in St. Louis, Missouri. Regarded as one of the great icons of horror cinema, Price built a career playing sinister, theatrical villains with a distinctive voice and commanding screen presence. He appeared in numerous classic horror films including House of Wax, The Fly, and a long series of Edgar Allan Poe adaptations for director Roger Corman. Beyond horror, he was also a noted art collector and cookbook author. He passed away on October 25, 1993, in Los Angeles, California.

name: James Stewart
bio: James Stewart was born on May 20, 1908, in Indiana, Pennsylvania. One of the most beloved actors in Hollywood history, Stewart was known for his natural, everyman acting style and distinctive drawl. He won the Academy Award for Best Actor for The Philadelphia Story in 1940 and received an honorary Oscar in 1985. He starred in many classic films including Mr. Smith Goes to Washington, Rear Window, Vertigo, and It's a Wonderful Life. He served as a bomber pilot in World War II, rising to the rank of Brigadier General. He passed away on July 2, 1997, in Beverly Hills, California.

name: Frank Sinatra
bio: Frank Sinatra was born Francis Albert Sinatra on December 12, 1915, in Hoboken, New Jersey. Regarded as one of the greatest entertainers of the 20th century, Sinatra had a legendary career as both a singer and an actor. He won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor for From Here to Eternity in 1953. As an actor he was known for taking on gritty, complex roles, most notably in The Man with the Golden Arm, for which he received a Best Actor nomination. As a singer, his recordings with Capitol Records in the 1950s are considered some of the finest vocal performances in popular music history. He passed away on May 14, 1998, in Los Angeles, California.
movies: The Man with the Golden Arm

name: Fred Astaire
bio: Fred Astaire was born Frederick Austerlitz on May 10, 1899, in Omaha, Nebraska. Widely considered the greatest dancer in the history of film, Astaire revolutionized the movie musical with his athletic, elegant style and his insistence on filming dance numbers in single, uncut takes. He partnered most famously with Ginger Rogers across ten films at RKO Pictures. He received an honorary Academy Award in 1950 and a nomination for Best Supporting Actor for The Towering Inferno in 1975. He passed away on June 22, 1987, in Los Angeles, California.

name: George A. Romero
bio: George Andrew Romero was born on February 4, 1940, in New York City. Romero is considered the father of the modern zombie film, having created the template for the genre with his landmark 1968 debut Night of the Living Dead, which he made independently on a shoestring budget in Pittsburgh. The film used its horror premise as sharp social commentary on race relations and civil unrest in America. He went on to direct Dawn of the Dead and Day of the Dead, cementing his legacy as one of the most influential genre filmmakers of all time. He passed away on July 16, 2017.

name: Adolphe Menjou
bio: Adolphe Menjou was born on February 18, 1890, in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. A polished and debonair character actor, Menjou was known for playing sophisticated, often cynical supporting roles across both silent and sound films. He received an Academy Award nomination for Best Actor for The Front Page in 1931. He appeared in over 100 films across a career spanning four decades. He passed away on October 29, 1963, in Beverly Hills, California.

name: Donna Reed
bio: Donna Reed was born Donna Belle Mullenger on January 27, 1921, in Denison, Iowa. She won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress for From Here to Eternity in 1953, playing against her wholesome image in a dramatic role. She is perhaps best remembered today for her role as Mary Hatch Bailey in It's a Wonderful Life alongside James Stewart. She later starred in her own long-running television series, The Donna Reed Show, from 1958 to 1966. She passed away on January 14, 1986, in Beverly Hills, California.

movies: The Man with the Golden Arm
